item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dont slurp pathos, shit sucks.
I dedicate this milestone celebration post to poor sweet little Timmy, without whom none of this would be possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://lmgtfy.com/?q=pathos+definition&s=g

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Pathos" is defined as "a quality that evokes pity or sadness". Imagine reading an article that is meant to make you feel lonesome or sad of sympathetic towards the characters involved. Think of a movie where the most important people do not get a happy ending.

Now, make that into water. This is evidently what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm missing something. What does "pathos" or "liquid pathos" mean in this context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally like to read SCPs that are straight to the point, at least sometimes. Not every one needs to be some grandiose tale with ten miles of addendum. It feels like a containment file doing it's job as intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my script writing days, I used to be the guy on the team who said "Okay, how can we make this *worse* for the character though? Can we get the audience to cry?" This really brought me back to those days, and I cackled all the way through the ride. Absolutely loved it. +1

item.title: Waap Waap Waap Waaaaaaaaaaa
item.content:encoded:
This article is basically the foundation equivalent of the sad trombone sound effect

+1

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
Nice one.

Tanhony rambles
TURN IT OFF

This is a nice bit of black humour - and I have to agree with you when you say that this only really works as well as it does because of the shorter length.

A few things that popped out at me:

identified by Foundation metaphysicians as impure liquid pathos

Love this. I’m always really into these tiny worldbuilding implications that the Foundation has a pretty good understanding of how things work on a metaphysical level.

Due to the nature of pathos, these restructurings are invariably harmful (physically or emotionally) to the subject consuming the fluid.

It’s a little cheeky not to go into the specifics of what these restructurings do, but I’m willing to forgive it for the sake of the story here.

three further family pets

The absurd number of dead pets here is great - it really helps push the events into the realm of tragically hilarious instead of just tragic.

All in all, an enthusiastic thumbs up!

+1

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to Tanhony or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I misread it to.
Might need to be reworded…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That looked boring at first, but then the end happened. T h e e n d .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even the authorities committed suicide.

edit: I think I misread it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't even know what kind of changes to reality it would make. The description is too vague and it leaves behind a document that's supposed to explain what it does, but it just leaves it hanging with nothing to add.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting anomaly, and clever story, but the execution felt underwhelming. The twist at the end felt a bit cheap, and it feels like you're going to your "old well" of dead kids to evoke tragedy.

Well written as always, but this one is a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The numero sign is more prevalent in British English than the hash symbol when it comes to representing the word "number". Since both dialects are ostensibly equal1 on the site, I've decided to stay true to my roots with stylistic choices and SPaG.

Footnotes
1. Although you lot have a tendency to be overzealous with corrections ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Metaphysician is a real word! It means, predictably, someone who studies metaphysics — an expert in the nature of reality and the relationship between thought and being. I felt it was appropriate here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… I can only describe as Kafkaesque. It’s funny until you remember it’s tragic. I upvoted, but would like to criticize that I feel “metaphysician” could be replaced with “metaphysicist”. I personally headcanon that a Metaphysics Division of the Foundation would study reality-bending though, so maybe pataphysicist or ‘pataphysicist1 would work better for cross-article cohesion, as the Foundation’s (‘)Pataphysics Division are the ones handling the narrativic stuff.

Footnotes
1. that’s the official spelling, with the apostrophe, look it up, probably a glottal stop, I don’t like it either

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Item №:

Excuse me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The chances of the characters and their situation getting progressively worse over time, only to end up with a literal fortune that has a one-in-a-million chance of happening, really suspends my disbelief to the point of believing it ever occurred.

Million-to-one chances crop up nine times out of ten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this… is this… god dammit. This is a fucking main lister based on an "X days from retirement" joke, and I both love and hate how well executed this is. Take my +1 and get out.

This might be because of me reading lots of poorly written "tragic" character biographies for RP projects in the past, but I find this piece quite charming. It delivers this incredibly over the top story just right, and I'm not bothered by it being so direct. Maybe it could've been better, but I think it works fine as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it didnt just happen. The anomaly changed reality for it too happen in its own, tragic, image. Of course all of these things are completely over the top and unlikely, thats why they are anomalous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Feels very Season 1-ish
I like it, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Pedantique; the circumstances that follow up to the family's death and at the narrative's end feel like it all led to one big unsatisfactory conclusion.

I like the anomaly, but you don't explicate it enough to the point that it seems you've thrown it aside in favor of (in my opinion anyway) a rather weak story of tragedy that ends up with the disappointment of the reader; particularly towards the really stretched out "Series of Unfortunate Events" that precede the rather jarring situational irony at the end.

In short: The chances of the characters and their situation getting progressively worse over time, only to end up with a literal fortune that has a one-in-a-million chance of happening, really suspends my disbelief to the point of believing it ever occurred.

That said, it's a close novote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly the perfect length. Any longer and the joke would wear thin.

item.title: Wha
item.content:encoded: Ok, 36 minutes of a concept and done in an hour? Damn, that's some of the fastest works of art i've seen completed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trying something new is always worthwhile! I'm not sure it would work at all if it was positively identified as liquid pathos at the very end either, that kind of reveal always feels like a bit too much, but I think it would be more interesting if that nature of it was left a bit more ambiguous. People seem to be enjoying it despite my quibbles though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig it, but this makes me wonder:

The farmland housing SCP-4763 has been encircled with a chain-link fence and CCTV system. No sapient entities are permitted access.

Are there any guards? Who responds to potential intruders? Is the CCTV monitered, or there for looks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a while to catch on — the double-take of "wait a minute, The Foundation wouldn't care about X" gave me a nice little journey!

Just outright calling it "liquid pathos" without some sort of sciencey-sounding acronym (I'm looking at you, "Apex-Tier Pluripotent Entity") strains credibility a bit for the Foundation, but that's a small quibble. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my mind, this only works as a piece of fiction because it's short. If I strung it out longer, the concerns of people like Pedantique would become much more prevalent — the more buildup you have, the more payoff you need. I could include Document 4763-Aeschylus, and detail all the different types of tragedy that have been induced, and the exact limits and parameters of its effects, but it just wouldn't be enjoyable for the reader to read — the absurd quantity of pathos only works once. If I strung it out more, it would become even more predictable than it is already.

I understand that it's fairly bare-bones and short, and it's perfectly fair to want some expansion on the anomaly; I don't think that makes it unfinished.

EDIT: Also, just for the record: I wasn't originally aiming to write this quickly. I just wanted to pin the idea to metaphorical paper as soon as possible. As I was finishing I realised "I can't think of anything I could do to make this better", so I posted. Speed and brevity were both happy side-effects of the initial inspiration, but not the ultimate goal at any stage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is hilariously sad. +1 for making me giggle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, it's a sad version of Lassie, and what else? I feel it unfinished, I don't mean it has to be a very long article to be good, but I felt kind of dissapointed at the end. You don't actually see the Document 4763-Aeschylus. I don't say that your SCP is bad, as I said before, I feel it unfinished. I understand that you wrote it in a record time, but I don't think that a SCP has to be written that way.

Again, as I said, I don't think is bad, only unfinished.

item.title: Legendary
item.content:encoded: You actually did it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't love the anomaly, and I agree with some of what Pedantique said above. This feels very similar to 4465, and the execution is fairly underwhelming, however the sheer emotion I felt while reading swings this into upvote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a perfectly valid criticism. While writing, I mentally flipped a coin over whether to have the crux of the article be the reveal of pathos, or the pathos itself. It metaphorically came up tails, so I went with the latter.

Also, I've been writing a lot of "sudden reveal at the end" type of anomalies, and so have a lot of other short-form authors. I wanted to try something different. Same with the identification of the pathos — it's a departure from most other conceptual scips that either I or others have written, which tend to explain these things in more detail.

Either way, it was an experiment, so I expected some negative reactions. Thanks for taking the time to look it over, and doubly so for posting in the discussion page; I appreciate comments much, much more than upvotes :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the concept, but outright stating that it's liquid pathos sort of undercuts the rest of the article for me (despite also enjoying the concept of the Foundation easily identifying something as liquid pathos). The subsequent events came off as "yeah, that sounds about right" instead of offering any kind of revelation at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very, very cleverly done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me? Yes. On the site? Probably not, but I'm gonna say it is until someone proves me wrong ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap. Is that a record?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 36 minutes from concept to posting let's go
